Q1 — Place: My grandfather's tool shed at the back of his garden in the village. The walls were lined with old chisels and saws hung on bent nails, each tool a different shade of brown from years of handling. The floor was a layer of sawdust that puffed up when you walked. The whole shed smelled of pine resin and machine oil and a slight smokiness from the wood-burning stove he kept in the corner. I used to stand there for hours just running my hand along the wood of his workbench, feeling the grain where he'd worn it smooth over decades. Each plank had a personality.
Q2 — Habit: I cannot pass a piece of furniture without checking how the joints are made. I'll be at someone's house and I'll subtly reach under the table to feel whether the apron is mortised in or just screwed and glued. I tap drawer fronts to hear if they're solid or veneered. My friends find this rude; I think of it as listening to whether something was made with care. I also collect wood scraps from construction sites without telling anyone. I have a drawer of small offcuts — oak, walnut, cherry, pine — that I just like to take out and hold.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain why a wooden chair leg eventually wobbles. People think the leg loosens. What actually happens: wood breathes with the seasons. In humid months it swells; in dry months it shrinks. The mortise-and-tenon joint that holds the leg has to accommodate this constant expansion and contraction. Over years, the wood fibers that make the joint tight compress permanently. The hole gets fractionally too big. The leg starts to rock. The fix isn't gluing — it's wedging in a sliver of fresh wood across the grain so the joint can find a new equilibrium with the seasons.
Q4 — Conflict: I had a falling-out with a contractor my partner hired to build us a bookshelf. The shelves were going to be hardwood, and he was treating them like a carpentry job — cut to length, screwed together, done by Friday. I kept asking about the grain direction on each shelf, whether the boards would be quarter-sawn or flat-sawn, how he'd account for seasonal movement. He thought I was being difficult and impossible to please. I was actually trying to spare him from building something that would warp in two winters. He built it his way. It warped in two winters.
Q5 — Sound: The sound of a sharp hand plane peeling a long curl of wood. Not the noise of the blade — the subtle sound of the wood itself giving way along a perfect line. It's almost a sigh. If the blade is dull or the angle wrong, the sound becomes a tear or a chatter. I learned to listen for the right sound long before I understood what was technically happening. The wood tells you whether you've found the grain or you're fighting it. The right sound is the wood's permission.
Q6 — Body: I was holding a board to cut, and my hand suddenly tightened and stopped me before my eyes had registered what was wrong. There was a knot dead in the line of the cut that my hand had felt through the wood — a small density change my fingertips picked up. Cutting through it would have kicked the blade back. I noticed about a half-second later, when my eyes finally caught what my hand had already known. The knot was barely visible from the surface; my hand had felt the deep structure of the board before I could see it.
Q7 — Unworded: The feeling of two pieces of wood fitting together with the right resistance. Not loose. Not tight. The exact amount of friction that means you've matched their geometry perfectly. When it slides home, there's this small soft click that's not really a sound — it's more like the wood saying yes. I've spent a long time trying to describe this to people. It's about a kind of correctness that lives in the materials themselves, that you can feel through your hands when you've gotten it right.
